original_abstract: The separation of dinitrogen (N2) from methane (CH4) is a critical industrial challenge for natural gas purification, rendered difficult by the similar physical properties of the two gases. In this work, we employ a suite of quantum mechanical computational methods, including density functional theory (DFT) and high-level correlated wave function theory, to predict the performance of a novel metal-organic framework, V-MOF-74. Our calculations reveal that the open V(II) sites within the framework provide a unique electronic environment that favors N2 over CH4 through enhanced π back-bonding. While both gases exhibit similar binding characteristics in the experimentally known Fe-MOF-74, the d3 electronic configuration of V(II) allows for significant electron donation into the unoccupied π* orbitals of N2, resulting in a much higher adsorption enthalpy. This selective interaction is further evidenced by significant N–N vibrational frequency shifts and detailed orbital analysis. These findings identify V-MOF-74 as a high-priority synthetic target for achieving efficient, thermodynamics-based dinitrogen separations.
humanized_abstract: Background We have seen from the previous examples that the separation of N 2 from CH 4 is extremely challenging due to very similar physical properties of the gases. In this study, we performed a number of computational studies using DFT and high-level correlated wave function theory to design a new potential material called V-MOF-74 and predicted its performance. Conclusions Based on our calculations, open V(II) sites in the structure of V-MOF-74 lead to preferential adsorption of N 2 over CH 4 by virtue of stronger π back-bonding. Although the two gases show a similar adsorption behavior in the known Fe-MOF-74 material, the d 3 electronic state of V 2+ allows V-MOF-74 to exhibit a higher affinity towards N 2 by providing extra electron density that is donated to the unoccupied π* orbitals of N 2, thereby causing a larger enhancement to the adsorption enthalpy. In addition, the enhanced adsorption of N 2 is supported by a significant increase in the N–N stretching vibrational frequencies and further evidence from the molecular orbital analysis. This suggests that V-MOF-74 is also worth synthesizing as a potential material to achieve thermodynamically favorable separation of N 2 from gas mixtures.
